data.title: Trump says soft inflation data mean deeper Fed rate cuts
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump again criticized Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, arguing that the softer December inflation data underscored the need for more aggressive interest-rate reductions.

The core Consumer Price Index rose less than expected both M/M (+0.2%) and Y/Y (+2.6%) in 2025's last month, meaning that "Jerome 'Too Late' Powell should cut interest rates, MEANINGFULLY!!! If he doesn't he will just continue to be, 'TOO LATE!'

The Fed has already shaved the policy rate by 75 basis points last year, although expectations for this month's Federal Open Market Committee decision are largely for a pause, potentially snapping a three-meeting streak of cuts.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Bausch Health sets 2025 outlook at the higher end of the prior range
data.description:
• Ahead of its presentation at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference this week, Bausch Health (BHC) on Tuesday set its full-year 2025 guidance at the higher end of the previously announced range, excluding its Bausch + Lomb (BLCO) subsidiary.  
  • In a regulatory filing, the Canadian pharma company said its management will issue several strategic and business updates at its JPM event scheduled to take place at 6:45 p.m. ET on January 14.
  • With its Q3 2025 financials, which beat Street forecasts in October, the Quebec-based company increased its full-year 2025 outlook for revenue and adjusted EBITDA to $10.05B-$10.25B and $3.57B-$3.66B, respectively.
  • Wall Street analysts currently expect Bausch Health (BHC) to report $3.91 of adjusted earnings per share on $10.18B in revenue for 2025.

data.title: Meta seeks to double output of Ray-Ban AI glasses -- report
data.description:
To capitalize on the growing demand for wearables, Meta (META) is reportedly in discussions with partner EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY) to double production of AI-enabled Ray-Ban smart eyeglasses by the end of 2026.

According to Bloomberg, Meta (META) and EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY) have discussed a production target of 20M units or more by the end of this year, with the potential to increase to more than 30M if needed.

EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY) is currently producing 10M Ray-Ban glasses annually.

The ambitious production target underscores Meta’s (META) commitment to increase investment in artificial intelligence and the company’s confidence in the widespread adoption of smart glasses. Last week, the company said it was delaying the international expansion of Ray-Ban Display glasses due to “limited inventory” and “unprecedented demand,” while reports circulated on  Monday that Meta (META) was reducing its metaverse staff by 10% to reallocate assets to the teams developing augmented reality and artificial intelligence.

By ramping up production of smart glasses, Meta (META) hopes to expand its lead in the smart glasses market over rival Google (GOOG). Through a $150M investment in Warby Parker (WRBY), Google (GOOG) is developing intelligent glasses intended for all-day wear. The Android XR-powered glasses will be available this year and will include audio-focused glasses and display glasses.

While the news has done little to bolster Meta (META) shares, EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY) and Warby Parker (WRBY) are both getting a lift with the former up 1.5% and the latter gaining more than 7% on speculation Google (GOOG) will match Meta’s (META) aggressive production goals.

data.title: Franklin Templeton broadens money market funds for stablecoins, blockchain
data.description:
Franklin Templeton (BEN) said Tuesday that two institutional money market funds managed by its affiliate Western Asset Management are now eligible for use in stablecoin reserve management and blockchain-enabled distribution markets.

Specifically, the update applies to two existing traditional Rule 2a-7 government money market funds -- the Western Asset Institutional Treasury Obligations Fund (LUIXX) and the Western Asset Institutional Treasury Reserves Fund (DIGXX).

The changes allow the funds to be used across two key segments of the emerging tokenized money market fund landscape, amid growing interest in digital delivery and tokenized access to traditional investment products.

“Traditional funds are already beginning to move on-chain, so rather than question their ability, our focus is to make them more accessible and useful by many,” said Roger Bayston, head of Digital Assets at Franklin Templeton. “By prioritizing interoperability and flexibility, we’re opening more ways for clients to access and deploy regulated funds across the platforms they rely on, giving investors greater choice in how they put their capital to work.”

A recent trend on Wall Street has been leveraging blockchain-based technologies to enhance traditional investment vehicles. BlackRock (BLK) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) are among some of the big firms embarking on tokenized money market funds.

data.title: NetApp initiated with Buy, while HP sees rating cut to Sell at Goldman
data.description:
Goldman restarted coverage of several companies across the IT Hardware and Distribution sector, including Dell Technologies (DELL), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), TD Synnex (SNX), and Penguin Solutions (PENG), with a Buy rating.

Hewlett Packard, which previously had a Neutral rating, has a price target of $31 at Goldman.

The firm assumed coverage of HP (HPQ) with a Sell rating, down from Neutral, with a $21 price target. Super Micro Computer (SMCI) rating was also restarted at Sell with a $26 price target.

Goldman restarted coverage of Ingram Micro (INGM) at Neutral, which previously had a Buy rating.

The firm began coverage of NetApp (NTAP) with a Buy rating and a $128 price target.

The analysts said that after lackluster returns in 2025 (+4% year-over-year versus SPX +16%), they expect the backdrop in 2026 will remain choppy as the industry navigates oscillating market enthusiasm for AI, concerns around higher input costs (e.g., DRAM/NAND), and the ongoing cyclical refresh of traditional IT tech stacks.

The analysts noted that while they proceed with caution, they believe investors will be rewarded for a patient approach, with the sector ripe for stock picking, with a preference for names where they see upside to consensus estimates and which they believe screen attractively against three key investor debates into 2026: Firstly, how sustainable is AI demand? Secondly, where are we in the refresh cycles for PCs, servers, storage, and campus networking? And thirdly, how will higher input costs impact margins and demand?

Goldman recommended investors: Buy Dell Technologies (DELL) for an AI-exposed EPS compounder with an operating model that should be well positioned to navigate margin headwinds. Buy Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) for business transformation with attractive upside. Buy NetApp (NTAP) for an EPS-compounder with an under-appreciated, high margin first-party public cloud offering. Buy TD Synnex (SNX) for a macro resilient distribution model with AI upside optionality from Hyve. Buy Penguin Solutions (PENG) for accelerating earnings growth from portfolio transformation.

The analysts noted that IT hardware stocks underperformed in 2025 as the industry navigated tariff headwinds, oscillating market enthusiasm for AI, and concerns around higher input costs. The analysts believe much of these headwinds will continue into 2026, informing their outlook for a mixed IT hardware demand environment.

On AI infrastructure demand, the analysts expect neocloud (GPU-as-a-service cloud computing) demand will remain strong, though product transitions and a widening XPU ecosystem may create some lumpiness from quarter to quarter. The analysts also expect to see continued traction in AI infrastructure investments at the enterprise and sovereign level, albeit still modest relative to neocloud.

On traditional servers and enterprise storage, Goldman is cautiously optimistic about revenue growth in 2026 driven by trends in data center modernization, though it will be monitoring the elasticity of demand against an inflated pricing environment, with the expectation that higher DRAM/NAND costs will be mostly passed along to customers.

On personal computers, or PCs, the analysts expect PC demand in 2026 will be weaker than the market is currently contemplating, driven by fading refresh tailwinds and higher pricing. The analysts anticipate the impact of higher input costs on margins and demand will be a key debate in 2026, and while it is still too early to assess the impact of price increases on the elasticity of demand, they believe there are opportunities to be selective on companies whose operating models and business mix are better positioned to navigate these headwinds.

data.title: Diageo evaluating options for China assets - Bloomberg
data.description:
Diageo (DEO) is considering options for its Chinese assets, including potential sales. 

Diageo (DEO) is working with Goldman Sachs and UBS Group AG to look at its operations, which include a more than 63% stake in Shanghai-listed Sichuan Swellfun Co., according to a  Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

The advisers are seeking initial interest from local strategic buyers and private equity firms, according to the report. 

Considerations are preliminary, and no final decisions have been made, Bloomberg reported.

Diageo, Goldman Sachs, and UBS declined to comment to Bloomberg. A representative for Sichuan Swellfun said the company hasn’t received any information about a stake sale.

Diageo ADRs fell 1%.

data.title: Pimco expects Fannie-Freddie MBS purchases to lower mortgage rates - report
data.description:
Last week, President Donald Trump's ordered U.S. mortgage giants Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to purchase $200B in mortgage-backed securities, in a move to lower mortgage rates to make homeownership more affordable.

Pacific Investment Management Co. Chief Investment Officer Daniel Ivascyn thinks the plan could help.

"We definitely think it could bring down rates and reduce the spread on mortgage-backed securities," he told Bloomberg News in an interview. The announcement provides "another tailwind" for Pimco's overweight holdings in the debt, which it has held through 2025, he added.

Trump's announcement “wasn’t the driver behind our ‘overweight’ decision, but it was always one potential catalyst for more aggressive spread tightening,” Ivascyn said.

In response to the administration's imminent purchase of MBSs, the Bloomberg MBS index gained 0.53% on Friday, its best single-day increase since the beginning of August, Bloomberg said.

Still, he points to the supply-demand mismatch at the root of the housing market affordability issue. "The long-term problem is that we have too few homes versus the number of households, and the only long-term solution will be to increase the housing stock," he said.

If spreads between MBSs and Treasuries tighten further, Pimco may reduce its overweight MBS holdings in the $217B Pimco income fund  "a bit" over time, Ivascyn said.

data.title: U.S. stocks slip as retail inflation comes in slightly cooler, earnings fail to lift sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Tuesday as investors assessed core CPI inflation, which came in slightly cooler than consensus, and some important earnings reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3% in late morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.7%.

The December Consumer Price Index came in at +0.3% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.7% YoY vs. +2.6% consensus and +2.7% prior.

Core CPI (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.6% YoY vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.

“Tuesday's CPI was in-line with expectations and that will likely keep the Federal Reserve on pause when it comes to interest rates for the foreseeable future… Recent positive employment data, elevated inflation, sticky price levels and political noise will keep the Fed at bay through at least the Spring,” said Skyler Weinand, CIO at Regan Capital.

“CPI is still under a cloud from the missing October number. Inflation is still relatively high, but trending downward. Consumers are still grappling with high overall price levels and sticker shock from the post-Covid jarring in housing, healthcare, education and everyday purchase prices,” Weinand added.

Also, new home sales slipped 0.1% MoM to 737K in October, vs. 716K expected, from 738K in the prior month, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Among companies reporting on Tuesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) -3% posted better-than-expected earnings, while Delta Air Lines (DAL) -1.5% had year-over-year revenue growth. 

Big companies reporting this week include Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Citigroup (C).

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was largely flat at 4.18%, while the U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also unchanged at 3.54%. Investors are awaiting a 30-Year bond auction later this afternoon.

data.title: Oppenheimer downgrades Adobe to Perform as demotions pile up
data.description:
Oppenheimer is the latest financial firm to downgrade Adobe, rerating the creative software company to Perform from Outperform.

"We had previously been bullish on Adobe as we expected its AI business momentum to reinvigorate growth in its Digital Media business," said Oppenheimer analysts, led by Brian Schwartz, in a detailed investor report on Tuesday. "But this did not play out as we expected, and is visible with Digital Media growth decelerating further in FY25."

Adobe shares were down 2.6% during early market action on Tuesday.

"In our view, Adobe has good medium-term opportunities and is a cheap stock," Schwartz said. "However, a challenging operating environment during the AI technology transition leading to uninspiring and decelerating top-line growth, inconsistent execution with product cycles, durability concerns about the moat, lackluster investor interest for owning software names, and down y/y operating margin guidance in FY26 will likely weigh negatively on the sentiment for the company's opportunities this year, and limit near-term upside for ADBE shares."

This is the latest downgrade to hit Adobe. Last week, BMO and Jefferies downgraded Adobe to Market Perform and Hold, respectively. This followed KeyBanc downgrading the stock to Underweight in mid-December.

It also reflects the broader trend of software names not experiencing the same boost as hardware names during the AI boom.

However, Oppenheimer has a few favorites when it comes to software, including Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW) and Agilysys (AGYS).

"We think MSFT's Azure results may look better in the 2H:CY26 when new data centers scale," Schwartz said. "NOW and CRM are a thematic play on the AI markets—companies needing to turn things around with investor sentiment (i.e., show-me stocks), and execute well on M&A. Lastly, AGYS should see reacceleration and material profit margin improvement in FY27 on the back of Marriott (MAR)."

data.title: St. Louis Fed's Musalem sees 'little reason' for further rate cuts
data.description:
St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem sees "little reason for further near-term easing," as he considers the Federal Reserve's current policy rate at "right around neutral," meaning the rate is neither fueling nor hindering the U.S. economy, he said on Tuesday.

Policy is in a good place to react in either direction, he added in a webcast sponsored by MNI. Excluding inflation, he estimates that the real policy rate is around 1%. It's "inadvisable" to put policy into an "accommodative stance, at this time," he noted.

There are some downside risks to the labor market and upside risks to inflation, he said. Still, Musalem expects the labor market to stabilize at current levels and inflation to progress toward the Fed's 2% goal.

For the year, he expects the U.S. economy to grow at or above potential this year, he said.

Musalem backed the Federal Reserve's December rate cut, citing some St. Louis Fed research that showed a lower proportion of firms have plans to hire this year and a higher proportion of firms have intentions to reduce their workforce.

There would be a reason to reduce the policy rate if the labor market weakens more than he expects and inflation declines below 2%. "That would allow policy to be eased, without policy going into accommodative territory," he added.

He's "hopeful" that the U.S. is in a higher productivity regime, but it's too early to tell, Musalem noted
